title: Long-range magnetic ordering and structural phase transition in disordered high-entropy spinel chromites
authors: Sushanta Mandal, Koushik Chakraborty, Isha, Arvind Kumar Yogi, S. D. Kaushik, Sourav Marik, Tirthankar Chakraborty
abstract: High-entropy spinel oxides provide an excellent platform for investigating entropy-stabilized correlated systems with strong configurational disorder. In this work, we systematically study the temperature evolution of the structural and magnetic properties of Cr-based high-entropy spinels with compositions $(Mn_{0.2}Co_{0.2}Ni_{0.2}Cu_{0.2}Zn_{0.2})Cr_2O_4$ and $(Mg_{0.2}Co_{0.2}Ni_{0.2}Cu_{0.2}Zn_{0.2})Cr_2O_4$. Our results reveal that both systems crystallize in cubic structure with space group \textit{$Fd\overline{3}m$} at room temperature. Each system undergoes antiferromagnetic ordering below the Néel temperatures $ T_N$ = 49 K and 35 K, respectively. Neutron diffraction measurements confirm the emergence of long-range magnetic order with spiral spin arrangement. Both systems exhibit a structural phase transition from cubic \textit{$Fd\overline{3}m$} to orthorhombic \textit{Fddd} symmetry at approximately 55 K and 85 K, respectively. Notably, despite the significant chemical disorder at the A site, both systems undergo transitions analogous to those observed in low entropy spinel systems. This behavior suggests that high configurational entropy may promote global structural stabilization despite local chemical disorder, thereby preserving long-range orderings and the characteristic symmetry-breaking transitions of the pristine spinel systems.
summary: This work investigates the structural and magnetic properties of disordered high-entropy spinel chromites. Using neutron and X-ray diffraction, the authors confirm that these materials exhibit both long-range antiferromagnetic ordering and structural phase transitions. The key finding is that these characteristic transitions persist despite high chemical disorder, suggesting that configurational entropy plays a role in stabilizing the ordered state.
main_problem: The study investigates the interplay between structural phase transitions and long-range magnetic ordering in disordered high-entropy spinel chromites.
main_result: Both high-entropy systems exhibit long-range magnetic order and undergo structural transitions to a lower symmetry, suggesting that high configurational entropy stabilizes the structure despite chemical disorder.
method: The research employed a combination of Powder X-ray Diffraction (P-XRD), Neutron Diffraction (NPD), and magnetic susceptibility measurements across varying temperatures.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Cr-based high-entropy spinel oxides, specifically $(	ext{Mn}_{0.2}	ext{Co}_{0.2}	ext{Ni}_{0.2}	ext{Cu}_{0.2}	ext{Zn}_{0.2})	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_4$ and $(	ext{Mg}_{0.2}	ext{Co}_{0.2}	ext{Ni}_{0.2}	ext{Cu}_{0.2}	ext{Zn}_{0.2})	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_4$. These materials are characterized by significant chemical disorder at the A-site.
key_observables: Structural symmetry (cubic $Fd ar{3}m$ to orthorhombic $Fddd$), Néel temperatures ($T_N$), lattice parameters, and magnetic Bragg reflections.
important_parameters: Transition temperatures ($\sim 55 	ext{ K}$ and $\sim 85 	ext{ K}$ for structure; $49 	ext{ K}$ and $35 	ext{ K}$ for magnetism); high configurational entropy regime.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed transitions are analogous to those in low-entropy spinels, and that the structural stabilization is promoted by high configurational entropy.
figures_summary: Figures show XRD/NPD peak splitting confirming symmetry reduction, temperature dependence of lattice parameters, and magnetic susceptibility measurements indicating antiferromagnetic ordering.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the temperature evolution of structural and magnetic properties using NPD and XRD. It establishes the room-temperature cubic phase, confirms long-range magnetic order below $T_N$, and details the structural phase transitions to the orthorhombic phase, concluding with the role of high entropy.
why_interesting: The persistence of long-range order and characteristic phase transitions in the presence of significant chemical disorder provides insights into how entropy can stabilize complex magnetic and structural ground states, which has implications for understanding correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Crystal structure of (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4. Here, the A-site (Mn, Ni, Co, Cu, Zn) occupies the tetrahedral position, and the B-site (Cr) occupies the octahedral position.

FIG. 2: X-ray diﬀraction patterns of (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 at (a) 300 K and (b) 11 K, and (Mg0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 at (c) 300 K and (d) 11 K. Insets in each graph show an enlarged view of the selec- tive peak splitting, where the (4 0 0) peak of the cubic phase splits into (0 4 0), (0 0 4), and (4 0 0) peaks of the orthorhombic phase.

Figure 7(left axis) shows the ﬁeld-cooled (FC) mag- netic susceptibility as a function of temperature, mea- sured under an applied magnetic ﬁeld of 0.1 T over the temperature range 4-100 K. The temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility shows a rapid increase at 49 K for (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 and at 35 K for (Mg0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 , corresponding to a paramagnetic to antiferromagnetic transition. The mag- netic transition temperatures are determined from the derivative dχ/dT and are presented in Figure 7(right axis). The sharp peak in dχ/dT provides estimated of TN, for 49 K and 35 K respectively. It is worth noting that in systems such as CuCr2O4 and NiCr2O4, which...

FIG. 3: Temperature evolution of XRD patterns for selected peaks of (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 and (Mg0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 : (a) The (3 1 1) reﬂection in the cubic phase splits into (1 1 3), (1 3 1), and (3 1 1) in the orthorhombic phase. (b) The (4 0 0) reﬂection in the cubic phase splits into (0 4 0), (0 0 4), and (4 0 0) in the orthorhombic phase of (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 . Similar peak splitting for (Mg0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 is shown in (c) and (d).

FIG. 4: (a) Rietveld reﬁnement of the neutron powder diﬀraction pattern at 3 K for (Mn0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2Zn0.2)Cr2O4 , showing observed intensity, calculated pattern, diﬀerence proﬁle, and Bragg positions corresponding to nuclear and magnetic reﬂections. (b) show the enlarged (4 0 0) reﬂection at 300 K as a single cubic peak (blue circle). At 3 K, this peak splits into orthorhombic (0 4 0), (4 0 0), and (0 0 4) reﬂections, accompanied by the appearance of magnetic Bragg peaks. This conﬁrms magnetically driven symmetry lowering from cubic to orthorhombic.
